title: 看看印度蓬勃发展的黑暗数据经济：跟踪软件和间谍软件应用程序，有关父母的数据库，有线电视客户，孕妇，共同基金投资者等等
title_en: A look at India's booming dark data economy: stalkerware and spyware apps, databases about parents, cable customers, pregnant women, mutual fund investors, more

note:
如果您正在阅读本文，那么您可能已经读到Elle上周末发表的多汁文章，记载了一位前彭博社记者在经历了长期报道后陷入了整个职业生涯-专业工具转变为定罪证券欺诈者马丁·史克里（Martin Shkreli）。如果您了解该文章，那么您可能也了解The Ad。
    随着Elle的故事广为传播，很明显，整个故事都针对大量的读者，并带有特定的广告……好吧，不同的人对此有不同的称呼，其中包括无袖睡衣和对接的连体衣。实际上，实际名称是“普通功能纽扣襟翼成人睡衣”，由一家名为IVRose的电子商务零售商以低至26.99美元的低价出售
 当我阅读Shkreli的熨平板时，看到onesie广告不少于六（！）次后，我的手机开始不停地嗡嗡作响，持续数小时之久不停地被其他遭到对接的轰炸机的读者嗡嗡作响。
  作为一个涵盖了广告定位古怪世界中最糟糕部分的人，Twitter用户每分钟都多次给我贴上标签，试图深入了解Onesie广告之谜。然后出现了少数几则DM和短信。事实证明，广告不仅淹没了Shkreli的故事，而且还淹没了人们的家乡报纸，食谱博客，以及几乎任何可以为错误的广告客户准备的可用房地产的互联网专区。
 在我花了更多时间仔细检查这个广告在何处出现之后，这些奇怪的广告看起来越险恶。实际上，到最后，我深信这些一体广告与以前从未听说过的一体零售商无关，而更多地是关于如何打破广告技术生态系统的希望。
    该广告甚至最终都出现在Elle上，更不用说在一篇文章中一遍又一遍了，这一事实证明了这种破裂的adtech生态系统。品牌顾问Nandini Jammi指出，onesie广告看似无所不在的一种流行理论是，大型广告客户经常使用第三方“品牌安全”技术使他们的品牌远离有害内容的可能性也误将Elle文章标记为“高端广告客户的“风险”或“不安全”。这些品牌的缺失留下了真空，低级广告客户（例如，曾经团结一网的电子商务品牌）可能不太在乎那些被视为不安全内容的杂物，从而填补了空白。
  当然，Shkreli的故事涉及一些漂亮的成人话题：离婚，节食，还有美国司法制度，仅举几例。但是通常，当广告商谈论“成人”内容时，他们是在谈论裸露的裸体，而不是一系列描述丈夫慢慢失去对已婚女人的控制的段落。 
为了深入了解这一点，我聘请了广告分析平台Adalytics的创始人Krzysztof Franaszek博士，他在周一就Mysterious Onesie的情况写下了自己的理论。具体来说，我希望他能帮助分析Shkreli故事通过其所谓的“标头出价”传递给潜在广告商的各种信号。
  您可能还记得最近撤销的德州– Google反托拉斯案例中的“标头出价”一词，但作为一个回顾：作为这些出价的一部分，从任何网站传递的标签通常旨在传达有关该网站广告空间的信息可能会举行。如果该内容偏向品牌不安全的领域，有时出价也会公开这些细节。
   根据Franaszek的分析，来自Shkreli作品的出价告诉了我们两件事：这个故事来自Elle的“热爱生活”行业，这是关于关系的长篇故事。与Elle合作的第三方品牌安全供应商与Oracle一起将该文章标记为讨论了很多有关“法律”的话题，但也指出，该故事并未讨论冠状病毒或“奇怪的是”动物之类的“不安全”主题。残酷。”
 出价数据还可以让我们查看由于某种原因将Shkreli的故事标记为不适合其广告的品牌。其中一些参与者包括万宝龙和香奈儿的珠宝分店等奢侈品零售商。虽然目前尚不清楚哪个关键词使这两个关键词更胜一筹，但值得一提的是，量身定制昂贵婚礼乐队的公司可能会觉得与Elle的故事相去甚远。
  在没有任何大牌竞争阻碍的情况下，IVRose显然投入了大量资金，希望在Elle的故事中让尽可能多的人看到它的连体衣。 Franaszek在CNBC上提到，当他对Shkreli故事进行一次小测试时，看起来IVRose每千次广告展示花费了10到12美元，这是大多数广告客户使用的通用基准。虽然这只比该品牌在Facebook之类的平台上支付的价格高出几美元，但当您定位到IVRose在这里瞄准的人数和频率时，这些钱就会迅速增加。
 换句话说，我们在Elle故事中看到的是墙对墙的联系，这表明该公司几乎在便宜货新闻功能上买断了所有广告空间。但是该策略有其自身的问题。
  也就是说，您为什么要花费数万美元（如果不是数十万美元）来​​宣传一套（看上去很舒适的）中档buttflap睡衣？ 
唯一能够对这个难题做出确切答案的人可以在IVRose团队的某个地方工作，而该公司没有回应我们（或实际上是任何一家媒体）的置评请求。
  没有他们的投入，我们能做的最好的事情就是读取IVRose分散在其站点上的跟踪和定位茶叶。值得庆幸的是，我们已经有人为我们做了那些肮脏的工作。经营分析咨询公司Victory Medium的博客之友Zach Edwards发布了一个Twitter帖子，解释说，任何可能的标志都尖叫着onesie广告从一开始就没有与onesies有关。
 当您查看从IVRose网站分支出来的广告合作伙伴的网络时，以及您记得他在推特上发布的“广告网络〜分享〜数据以$$”时，您不难相信IVRose花费了大笔现金广告空间实际上是花在Elle读者生成的超有价值数据桶上的。再加上一个事实，即现在，这些类别的读者可能比平时更有价值，因为好吧，美国历史上一直在假期中花费自己的现金。
  正如爱德华兹（Edwards）在其Twitter话题中指出的那样，要弄清楚IVRose计划如何处理几乎可以肯定的潜在潜在客户数据集，是不可能的。实际上，有一个技术用语：“ cookie同步”。描述该过程的最简单方法是在一组合作的adtech平台之间进行某种握手，该协议可让安排两侧的人们来回交换特定的用户数据集。但这是我们正在谈论的adtech，这意味着过程不必要地复杂，可能是我们每个人在聚会上都想谈的最后一件事。
 同时，这也是一种很卑鄙的策略，它保证我们对那些无聊的睡衣的点击不会在开玩笑，而在我们的数字生活中可能会继续困扰我们。
  据爱德华兹说，这一切都始于所讨论的那个人的登陆页面。加载该页面时，它会触发一家名为Clientgear的公司的一项特定跟踪技术，该公司本身是YeahMobi的子公司，后者是一家总部位于中国的受欢迎的广告技术公司。 Clientgear的角色是双重的：首先，如果您从未访问过碰巧是其合作伙伴之一的网站，则需要为您的特定设备创建唯一的Clientgear专用ID。在将该ID的副本存储到其网络上之后（以防万一您返回到onesie页面并需要重新标识），该标识符会广播给其他广告技术中间商，因为YeahMobi恰好与之建立了某种业务关系。在这种情况下，在美国和中国似乎有不少于15家公司符合这一要求。
 为了交换他们刚从onesie页面获得的闪亮的新用户ID，这些伙伴中的每一个都传递自己的标识符作为交换。有时候，如果是过去某个时候碰巧与您发生冲突的第三方，那么它已经拥有大量描述您的身份的数据。无论如何，一旦Clientgear和另一端的合作伙伴正式为您“同步”他们的用户ID，通常的计划是继续交换任何cookie数据，要么有人可以从您那里窃取，直到您告诉他们退出它。 
在某些情况下，即使您确实告诉他们退出（例如清除浏览器的Cookie缓存），研究人员也可以回溯到2014年，他们注意到，一些苗条的合作伙伴可以结合使用自己的数据存储库和其他基本数据从字面上“重生”曾经存在于另一端的cookie。如果您好奇的话，官方的术语是“ evercookie”，我保证，它听起来并不美味。
   另外，我也不想成为向您破门的人，但是，如果您对这些法兰绒睡衣的想法感到困惑，那么它们实在是无能为力。当然，您可以对浏览器的Cookie缓存进行深度清理，但是任何可衡量的效果都可以很快撤消。正如上面的研究小组所述，从您的浏览器缓存中清除Cookie或任何关联的用户ID并不意味着这些十几个合作伙伴中的任何一个都有任何理由这样做。当您踏入一个试图与其预先存在的标签之一进行同步的网站的那一刻，您尝试清除的所有内容（包括被诅咒的一个人）都将被链接回您创建的任何新ID，从而使您几乎回到你开始的地方。
  这样一来，无人连身衣的广告就可能成为关键所在，从而导致您的数据在多个大洲的十几个影子技术公司之间永远地被打乱。当然，可能很难相信，一个具有无限记忆力的广告永远都不会扭转我们的命运。但是，至少在发生这种情况时，我们有现成的服装。
note_en:
If you’re reading this, you might have read the   juicy piece that Elle dropped this weekend chronicling how a former Bloomberg reporter torched her entire career after falling for the longtime subject of her reporting—  professional-tool-turned-convicted-securities-fraudster Martin Shkreli. And if you know about that article, you probably know about The Ad.
    As Elle’s story went viral, it became clear that a ton of the readers were all being targeted en masse across the story with a specific ad for... well, different people had different names for it,   assless pajamas and   buttflapped onesies among them. In fact, the actual name is “  Plain Functional Buttoned Flap Adults Pajamas,” and they’re sold by an e-commerce retailer named IVRose for the low, low price of $26.99
 After seeing the onesie ad no less than six(!) times while I read the Shkreli love screed, my phone started buzzing ceaselessly for quite literally hours on end by other readers who were bombarded with butt.
  As someone who covers the   worst parts of the wacky world of ad targeting, I was getting tagged multiple times per minute by Twitter users trying to get to the bottom of the Onesie Ad Mystery. Then came the handful of DM’s and text messages. As it turns out, the ad didn’t only swallow the Shkreli story, but people’s   hometown papers,   recipe blogs, and just about any internet corner with available real estate ready for the errant advertiser.
 After I spent more time scrutinizing how, exactly, this ad turned up everywhere, the more sinister these weird advertisements started to look. In fact, by the end of things, I became convinced that these onesie ads were less about a onesie retailer no one had heard of before, and more about how hopelessly broken the adtech ecosystem is.
    The fact that this ad even ended up on Elle at all, let alone over and over again in a single article, is evidence of this busted adtech ecosystem. One popular theory for the onesie ad’s seeming omnipresence,   pointed out brand consultant Nandini Jammi, was the possibility the third-party “brand safety” tech that big advertisers often use to keep their brands away from icky content erroneously marked the Elle article as too “risqué” or “unsafe” for top-end advertisers. The absence of those brands left a vacuum that lower-tier advertisers (such as onesie-hawking e-commerce brands) that likely didn’t care as much about being seen next to a smidge of unsafe content, could fill.
  For sure, the Shkreli story deals with some pretty adult topics: divorce, gaslighting, and, well, the American justice system, just to name a few. But generally, when advertisers talk about “adult” content, they’re talking about   hardcore nudity, not a series of paragraphs describing a husband slowly losing his grip on the woman he married.
 To get to the bottom of this, I corralled Dr. Krzysztof Franaszek, founder of the ad analytics platform Adalytics, who had written out   his own theories on the Mysterious Onesie situation on Monday. Specifically, I wanted his help analyzing the sorts of signals that the Shkreli story was passing off to potential advertisers through its so-called “header bids.”
  You might remember the phrase “header bids” from the recently dropped   Texas–Google antitrust case, but just as a refresher: The tags that get passed from any site as part of these bids are generally meant to convey something about the ad space that site might hold. If that content veers toward brand-unsafe territory, sometimes the bid   discloses those details as well.
   Per Franaszek’s analysis, the bids coming from the Shkreli piece told us two things: This story was coming from Elle’s   “life-love” vertical, and it was a long-form story about relationships. A third-party brand safety vendor that Elle partners with, Oracle, marked the piece as discussing a lot about “law,” but also noted that this story didn’t discuss “unsafe” topics like the coronavirus or—strangely enough—“animal cruelty.”
 The bid data also let us look at the brands that, for one reason or another, marked the Shkreli story as unsuitable for their ad bucks. Some of these players included luxury retailers like Montblanc and Chanel’s jewelry branch. While it’s unclear which keywords in the piece tripped these two up, it’s worth assuming that companies that tailor-make   expensive wedding bands might feel out of place advertising alongside Elle’s story.
  Without any big-name competition holding it back, IVRose apparently poured a ton of money into getting its onesies seen by as many people as possible on Elle’s story. Franaszek mentioned   to CNBC that when he ran a small test on the Shkreli story, it looked like IVRose was spending between $10 to $12 dollars per thousand ad impressions, the common benchmark that most advertisers use. While that’s only a few dollars more than the brand would be paying on, say, a platform   like Facebook, those dollars quickly add up when you’re targeting as many people—and as frequently—as IVRose seems to be doing here.
 In other words, the fact that we were seeing wall-to-wall onesies in the Elle story was indicative of a company pretty much buying out all of the ad spaces on a bargain-bin news feature. But that strategy comes with its own questions.
  Namely, why would you spend tens of thousands—if not hundreds of thousands— of dollars to advertise an (admittedly kinda cozy-looking) set of medium-grade buttflap pajamas?
  The only ones with any definitive answer to that puzzle work somewhere on IVRose’s team—and the company didn’t respond to our (or really, any outlet’s) request for comment.
  Without their input, the best we can do is read the tracking-and-targeting tea leaves that IVRose has scattered across its site. Thankfully, we have someone that did that dirty work for us already. Friend of the blog Zach Edwards, who runs the analytics consulting firm Victory Medium, posted a   Twitter thread explaining that every possible sign screams the onesie ads were never about onesies to begin with.
 When you look at the web of ad partners branching out of the IVRose site—and when you remember that “ad networks ~share~ data for $$,” as he tweeted—it’s not hard to believe that the buckets of cash IVRose was spending on ad space was actually being spent on the buckets of super-valuable data generated by Elle readers. Add in the fact that, right now, these buckets of readers are likely more valuable than they’d typically be, since, well, the U.S. has historically spent   its own buckets of cash during the holiday season.
  As Edwards   pointed out in his Twitter thread, it’s not impossible to figure out what IVRose plans to do with what is almost assuredly boatloads of data from potential onesie customers. In fact, there’s a technical phrase for it: “cookie synching.” The easiest way to describe the process is something of a handshake between a set of partnering adtech platforms that lets folks on both sides of the arrangement swap specific sets of user data back and forth. But this is adtech we’re talking about—which means the process is   needlessly complicated and probably the last thing any of us would want to talk about at a party.
 At the same time, it’s also a deeply shitty tactic that promises our clicks on those assless pajamas will—no joke—likely keep on haunting us for the rest of our digital lives.
  According to Edwards, it all starts with the landing page for the onesie in question. As that page loads, it triggers a specific piece of tracking tech from a company called Clientgear—itself a subsidiary of the   YeahMobi, a popular adtech player based out of China. Clientgear’s role here is twofold: First, if you’ve never visited a site that happened to be one of its partners, it needs to create a unique, Clientgear-specific ID for your particular device. After tucking a copy of that ID on its network (just in case you return to the onesie page and need to be re-identified), that identifier gets broadcast to the miscellaneous adtech middlemen that YeahMobi happens to have some sort of a business relationship with. In this case, it seems like there are   no fewer than 15 companies in the U.S. and China that fit this bill.
 In exchange for the shiny new user ID that they just got from the onesie page, each of these partners pass back their own identifier in exchange. Sometimes, if it’s a third party that happened to cross paths with you sometime in the past, it already has troves of data describing who you are. In any case, once Clientgear and the partnering player on the other end officially “sync” their user ID’s for you together, the usual plan is to continue swapping any cookie-derived data either one can siphon off of you  until you tell them to quit it.
  In some cases, even if you  do tell them to quit it—by, say, clearing out your browser’s cookie cache—researchers as far back as 2014   have noted that some of the slimier partners can use their own data stores combined with other basic data to literally “respawn” the cookie that once existed on the other end. The official lingo for that, in case you were curious, is an “  evercookie,” which is, I promise, not as tasty as it sounds.
   Also, I hate to be the one to break it to you, but if you’re creeped out at the idea of these flannel pajamas stalking you in some shape or form in perpetuity, there really isn’t much you can do. Sure, you can do a   deep scrub on your browser’s cookie cache, but any measurable effect can be undone pretty quickly. As the research team above noted, clearing out the cookies or any associated user IDs from your browser cache doesn’t mean that any of these dozen-plus partners have any reason to do the same. The minute that you stumble onto a website that tries to sync with one of their pre-existing tags, everything that you tried to scrub—including that cursed onesie—will be linked back to whatever new ID you’ve created, putting you pretty much back where you started.
  So that’s how an ad for an assless onesie could be the lynchpin that results in your data being eternally shuffled between more than a dozen shadowy tech players across multiple continents. Sure, it might be hard to believe that an ad for something so infinitely meme-able would ever turn around and bite us in the ass at all. But at least we have a ready-made outfit for when that happens.
title: 有关数字广告标头出价，第三方品牌安全销售商和“ evercookies”的解释器，以无处不在的睡衣onesie广告为例
title_en: An explainer on digital advertising's header bids, third-party brand safety vendors, and “evercookies”, illustrated by a ubiquitous pajama onesie ad

国会批准等待唐纳德·特朗普总统签名的两项法案将限制迈克尔·派克的权力，迈克尔·派克是总统领导美国全球媒体代理的选择，该机构负责监管美国之音和其他四个国际媒体组织。
 周一晚些时候，国会批准了一项用于COVID-19救援和美国政府行动的大规模支出措施，以及先前批准的《国防授权法》都包括限制美国通用汽车公司及其首席执行官权力的变化，但采取了不同的方式。
 一起的两个法案可能会限制现在和1月20日之间的包的动作，当民主党总统当选人拜登上任。如果拜登决定更换Pack，它将在最初三个月为该人提供人员变更方面的更多余地。
 例如，众议院和参议院周一批准的支出措施包括一项条款，该条款将给予开放技术基金会更多的时间来回应美国通用汽车公司的一封信，该信提议拒绝互联网自由基金提供额外的联邦资金。
 帕克在他的提议中切断了涉嫌的利益冲突和涉嫌违规行为，该提议是切断未来向非营利组织提供的联邦资金，该组织将帮助支持全世界的互联网访问。
  互联网自由组织说，USAGM提议取消OTF资格可能会拒绝联邦政府为非营利组织提供三年资助，并切断记者和激进分子的生命线
 庞大的支出立法还将暂时暂停90天的NDAA规定，以强制要求成立新的USAGM咨询委员会和有关如何招聘和解雇网络负责人的法规。 
如果两份法案都由总统签署，并且拜登提名USAGM的新领导人，那么Pack的继任者将拥有更广泛的权力，可以在任期开始时任命和罢免网络代理领导人。一旦为期90天的NDAA暂停期结束，新任首席执行官将需要获得大多数机构咨询委员会的批准，才能雇用或罢免网络领导者。
 一种在六月拜登竞选总统的发言人告诉记者的网站VOX，如果当选，拜登将驳回USAGM目前的头。
   在经过参议院长时间的确认战后于6月负责USAGM的Pack因一系列行动遭到议员批评，包括他在第一周解雇了三个网络部门负责人，试图从OTF扣留资金，以及无法续签J-1签证–具有独特技能的个人的专业入境许可，使国际新闻工作者能够在VOA的语言服务中工作。
 12月，Pack任命了VOA，欧洲自由电台/自由电台，古巴广播局，亚洲自由电台（RFA）和中东广播网（MBN）的新负责人。
  后两项任命于周二晚上宣布。国会和白宫的高级政策顾问Victoria Coates将担任MBN总裁，Stephen Yates则将担任MBN总裁，Stephen Yates曾担任美国副总统办公室副总统国家安全顾问兼美国国防部语言分析师。 RFA。
 包括众议院外交事务委员会主席埃利奥特·恩格尔（Eliot Engel）在内的议员们，以及来自德克萨斯州的共和党人迈克尔·麦考尔（Michael McCaul），都对某些选择的适用性表示了担忧。
  《国防授权法》中的其他规定包括禁止首席执行官或联邦雇员在专职董事会任职，以及要求董事会成员具有新闻，技术或外交方面的专门知识。 
周一批准的支出计划还明确规定，OTF有90天的时间应对拟议的取消资格，这将剥夺其联邦资金。在12月15日向OTF通知拟采取的行动的信中，USAGM给了受赠人30天的回复时间。
 Pack在信中援引了被聘用进行合规审查的私人律师事务所McGuireWoods所指称的利益冲突和违规行为，作为拟议的取消资格的原因。 OTF对指控提出异议。
 OTF主席劳拉·坎宁安（Laura Cunningham）告诉美国之音（VOA），此举是“通过劫持和武器化旨在保护公众利益的程序破坏OTF”。
  坎宁安告诉美国之音：“一个两党制，两党制的立法者团体能够在如此短的时间内在最高水平上采取行动，这是他们对保护互联网自由及其对OTF的支持的不可思议的证明。”
 根据USAGM网站的数据，全球有超过20亿人使用OTF支持的工具和技术，其中包括诸如Signal和Tor Project之类的流行加密工具。 OTF专注于开源技术，可帮助记者和激进主义者规避监视和审查。
  美国资助广播的领导人在特朗普政府的最后几周任命了三位新的网络负责人
    特别报告员办公室在举报人的投诉表明存在重大不法行为之后，命令美国通用汽车公司进行调查。
note_en:
Two bills approved by Congress awaiting President Donald Trump’s signature would limit the powers of Michael Pack, the president’s pick to lead the U.S. Agency for Global Media, which oversees Voice of America and four other international media organizations.
 A massive spending measure for COVID-19 relief and U.S. government operations approved late Monday by Congress and a previously approved National Defense Authorization Act both include changes limiting the powers of the USAGM and its chief executive, but in different ways.
 The two bills taken together could restrict Pack’s actions between now and January 20, when Democratic President-elect Joe Biden takes office. And if Biden decides to replace Pack, it would provide that person with more leeway in making personnel changes for the first three months.
 For example, the spending measures approved by the House and Senate on Monday include a provision that would give the Open Technology Fund more time to respond to a USAGM letter proposing that the internet freedom fund be denied additional federal funding.
 Pack cited alleged conflicts of interest and alleged irregularities in his proposal to cut off future federal funds to the nonprofit organization that helps support internet access throughout the world.
  USAGM proposal to debar OTF could deny federal funding to nonprofit for three years and cut lifeline to journalists and activists, internet freedom group says
 The massive spending legislation would also temporarily pause for 90 days NDAA provisions mandating the formation of new USAGM advisory boards and regulations for how network heads can be hired and fired.
 If both bills are signed by the president and if Biden nominates a new leader for USAGM, Pack’s successor would have broader powers to appoint and remove network agency leaders at the beginning of his or her tenure. Once the 90-day NDAA pause ends, the new CEO would need the approval of a majority of the agency advisory board to hire or remove network leaders.
 A spokesperson for the Biden presidential campaign in June told a reporter for the website VOX that, if elected, Biden would dismiss the USAGM’s current head.
   Pack, who took charge of USAGM in June after a prolonged Senate confirmation battle, has been criticized by lawmakers over a series of actions including his firing of the heads of three network divisions in his first week, attempts to withhold funds from the OTF, and failure to renew J-1 visas – a specialized entry permit for individuals with unique skills that allows international journalists to work in VOA’s language services.
 In December, Pack appointed new heads to VOA, Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty, the Office of Cuba Broadcasting, Radio Free Asia (RFA) and the Middle East Broadcasting Network (MBN).
  The latter two appointments were announced Tuesday evening. Victoria Coates, a senior policy adviser to Congress and the White House, will be president of MBN and Stephen Yates, a former deputy national security adviser in the Office of the Vice President and language analyst at the U.S. Department of Defense, will be president of RFA.
 Lawmakers, including House Foreign Affairs Committee chair Eliot Engel, a Democrat from New York, and ranking Republican Michael McCaul of Texas have raised concerns over the suitability of some of the choices.
  Other provisions in the National Defense Authorization Act include preventing the CEO or federal employees from serving on a grantee board and a requirement that board members have expertise in journalism, technology or diplomacy.
  The spending package approved Monday also explicitly gives the OTF 90 days to respond to the proposed debarment that would strip it of federal funding. In a  December 15 letter informing the OTF of the proposed action, USAGM gave the grantee 30 days to respond.
 In the letter, Pack cited alleged conflicts of interest and irregularities identified by McGuireWoods, a private law firm hired to carry out compliance reviews, as reason for the proposed debarment. OTF disputes the allegations.
 OTF president Laura Cunningham told VOA the move was an attempt to “destroy OTF by hijacking and weaponizing a process intended to protect the public interest.”
  “That a bicameral, bipartisan group of lawmakers was able to come together at the highest level on such a short timeline to take this action, is an incredible demonstration of their commitment to protect internet freedom and their support for OTF,” Cunningham told VOA.
 Over 2 billion people globally use tools and technologies supported by OTF, according to the USAGM website, including popular encryption tools like Signal and the Tor Project. With a focus on open-source technology, the OTF helps journalists and activists circumvent surveillance and censorship.

title: 国会已批准的两项法案将限制USAGM的Michael Pack的权力，包括给予开放技术基金会更多的时间来捍卫其预算
title_en: Two bills Congress has approved would limit the powers of USAGM's Michael Pack, including giving the Open Technology Fund more time to defend its budget

纽约州州长安德鲁·库莫（Andrew Cuomo）周二签署了一项法案，暂时禁止在学校中使用面部识别，这使其成为第一个采取这种步骤的州。
      该法律在公立和私立学校中暂停面部识别和其他生物识别技术，直到2022年7月，指示该州进行研究并就该技术是否适合学校提出建议。
  Cuomo的办公室表示，这项研究将“寻求老师和家长以及学校安全，保障，数据和学生隐私问题专家的反馈。”
  隐私组织批评洛克波特市学区在今年早些时候在所有K-12建筑物中部署面部识别之后，颁布了这项法律。该地区表示，这是一项有争议的工作，旨在更轻松地识别性犯罪者，未经授权的学校工作人员或其他可能危险。
  虽然少数几个城市禁止政府使用面部识别技术，包括在警察部门内部，但纽约是第一个禁止公立和私立学校都使用该技术的州。
  纽约的暂缓令并未解决警察使用面部识别的问题，这也是公民自由组织所关注的问题，并且并未彻底禁止使用该技术，从而有可能在研究完成后允许学校使用该技术。
    ＆＃34;人脸识别技术可以为纽约人带来很多好处，但是它的使用带来了我们必须检查的严重而合法的隐私问题，尤其是在学校中，＆＃34;库莫在一份声明中说。该立法要求州教育政策制定者退后一步，与专家协商并解决隐私问题，然后再确定是否可以将任何种类的生物识别技术带入纽约的学校。 
反对面部识别的运动，尤其是在K-12学校中，正在迅速发展。 公民自由倡导者表示，面部识别技术不够可靠，无法掌握在学校管理人员手中，尤其是因为研究表明，面部识别算法更容易误识别肤色较深的人。 但是，即使这项技术是100％准确的，批评家们说，滥用的可能性也很高，可以用来针对学生，以免违反小规矩或上课迟到。  我是旧金山的记者，报道《福布斯》的最新消息。 在Twitter @rachsandl上关注我，或给我发送电子邮件rsandler@forbes.com。
note_en:
New York Gov. Andrew Cuomo signed a bill Tuesday temporarily banning the use of facial recognition in schools, making it the first state to take such a step.
      The law suspends facial recognition and other biometric technology in both public and private schools until July 2022, directing the state to conduct a study and issue recommendations about whether the technology is appropriate for schools.
  Cuomo’s office said the study will “seek feedback from teachers and parents, as well as experts in school safety, security, data and student privacy issues.”
  The law was introduced after privacy groups criticized the Lockport City School District for  deploying facial recognition in all K-12 buildings earlier this year, a controversial effort the district said was intended to more easily identify sex offenders, unauthorized school staff or other adults that might be dangerous.
  While a handful of cities have banned government use of facial recognition, including inside police departments, New York is the first state to ban both public and private schools from using the technology.
  New York’s moratorium doesn’t address police use of facial recognition, which is also a concern for civil liberties groups, and it doesn’t ban the technology outright, leaving the possibility that schools could be allowed to use it after the study is completed.
    "Facial recognition technology could provide a host of benefits to New Yorkers, but its use brings up serious and legitimate privacy concerns that we have to examine, especially in schools," Cuomo said in a  statement. "This legislation requires state education policymakers to take a step back, consult with experts and address privacy issues before determining whether any kind of biometric identifying technology can be brought into New York's schools.
    The movement against facial recognition, especially in K-12 schools, is gaining steam. Civil liberties advocates say facial recognition technology isn’t reliable enough to be in the hands of school administrators, especially because research has shown facial recognition algorithms are more  likely to  misidentify people with darker skin. But even if the technology were 100% accurate, critics  say that the potential for abuse is too high and could be used to target students for minor rule breaking or being late to class.
  I’m a San Francisco-based reporter covering breaking news at Forbes. Follow me on Twitter @rachsandl or shoot me an email rsandler@forbes.com.
title: 纽约州州长签署一项法案，在学校中止使用面部识别和其他生物识别技术，直到2022年7月，这是美国的第一个此类法规
title_en: NY Governor signs a bill to suspend the use of facial recognition and other biometric ID tech in schools until July 2022, the first such regulation in the US
